sourceRefs.fallbackText: The following sections describe the standard types that are built into the interpreter.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The principal built-in types are numerics, sequences, mappings, classes, instances and exceptions.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The principal built-in types are numerics, sequences, mappings, classes, instances and exceptions.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Some collection classes are mutable. The methods that add, subtract, or rearrange their members in place, and don’t return a specific item, never return the collection instance itself but None .
sourceRefs.textQuote: Some collection classes are mutable. The methods that add, subtract, or rearrange their members in place, and don’t return a specific item, never return the collection instance itself but None .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Some operations are supported by several object types; in particular, practically all objects can be compared for equality, tested for truth value, and converted to a string (with the repr() function or the slightly different str() function). The latter function is implicitly used when an object is written by the print() function.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There are eight comparison operations in Python. They all have the same priority (which is higher than that of the Boolean operations). Comparisons can be chained arbitrarily; for example, x < y <= z is equivalent to x < y and y <= z , except that y is evaluated only once (but in both cases z is not evaluated at all when x < y is found to be false).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unless stated otherwise, objects of different types never compare equal. The == operator is always defined but for some object types (for example, class objects) is equivalent to is . The < , <= , > and >= operators are only defined where they make sense; for example, they raise a TypeError exception when one of the arguments is a complex number.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Unless stated otherwise, objects of different types never compare equal. The == operator is always defined but for some object types (for example, class objects) is equivalent to is . The < , <= , > and >= operators are

highSignalBlocks.text: Unless stated otherwise, objects of different types never compare equal. The == operator is always defined but for some object types (for example, class objects) is equivalent to is . The < , <= , > and >= operators are only defined where they make sense; for example, they raise a TypeError exception when one of the arguments is a complex number.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Non-identical instances of a class normally compare as non-equal unless the class defines the __eq__() method.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Non-identical instances of a class normally compare as non-equal unless the class defines the __eq__() method.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Instances of a class cannot be ordered with respect to other instances of the same class, or other types of object, unless the class defines enough of the methods __lt__() , __le__() , __gt__() , and __ge__() (in general, __lt__() and __eq__() are sufficient, if you want the conventional meanings of the comparison operators).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The behavior of the is and is not operators cannot be customized; also they can be applied to any two objects and never raise an exception.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There are three distinct numeric types: integers , floating-point numbers , and complex numbers . In addition, Booleans are a subtype of integers. Integers have unlimited precision. Floating-point numbers are usually implemented using double in C; information about the precision and internal representation of floating-point numbers for the machine on which y
sourceRefs.textQuote: There are three distinct numeric types: integers , floating-point numbers , and complex numbers . In addition, Booleans are a subtype of integers. Integers have unlimited precision. Floating-point numbers are usually imp

highSignalBlocks.text: There are three distinct numeric types: integers , floating-point numbers , and complex numbers . In addition, Booleans are a subtype of integers. Integers have unlimited precision. Floating-point numbers are usually implemented using double in C; information about the precision and internal representation of floating-point numbers for the machine on which your program is running is available in sys.float_info . Complex numbers have a real and imaginary part, which are each a floating-point number. To extract these parts from a complex number z , use z.real and z.imag . (The standard library includes the additional numeric types fractions.Fraction , for rationals, and decimal.Decimal , for floating-point numbers with user-definable precision.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Numbers are created by numeric literals or as the result of built-in functions and operators. Unadorned integer literals (including hex, octal and binary numbers) yield integers. Numeric literals containing a decimal point or an exponent sign yield floating-point numbers. Appending 'j' or 'J' to a numeric literal yields an imaginary number (a complex number
sourceRefs.textQuote: Numbers are created by numeric literals or as the result of built-in functions and operators. Unadorned integer literals (including hex, octal and binary numbers) yield integers. Numeric literals containing a decimal poi

highSignalBlocks.text: Numbers are created by numeric literals or as the result of built-in functions and operators. Unadorned integer literals (including hex, octal and binary numbers) yield integers. Numeric literals containing a decimal point or an exponent sign yield floating-point numbers. Appending 'j' or 'J' to a numeric literal yields an imaginary number (a complex number with a zero real part) which you can add to an integer or float to get a complex number with real and imaginary parts.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The constructors int() , float() , and complex() can be used to produce numbers of a specific type.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The constructors int() , float() , and complex() can be used to produce numbers of a specific type.

highSignalBlocks.text: The constructors int() , float() , and complex() can be used to produce numbers of a specific type.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There are eight comparison operations in Python. They all have the same priority (which is higher than that of the Boolean operations). sourceRefs.textQuote: There are eight comparison operations in Python. They all have the same priority (which is higher than that of the Boolean operations). Comparisons can be chained arbitrarily; for example, x < y <= z is equivalent to x <

sourceRefs.textQuote: This table summarizes the comparison operations:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Unless stated otherwise, objects of different types never compare equal. The == operator is always defined but for some object types (for example, class objects) is equivalent to is . The < , <= , > and >= operators are only defined where they make sense; for example, they raise a TypeError exception when one of the arguments is a complex number.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Unless stated otherwise, objects of different types never compare equal. The == operator is always defined but for some object types (for example, class objects) is equivalent to is . The < , <= , > and >= operators are

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Non-identical instances of a class normally compare as non-equal unless the class defines the __eq__() method.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Non-identical instances of a class normally compare as non-equal unless the class defines the __eq__() method.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Instances of a class cannot be ordered with respect to other instances of the same class, or other types of object, unless the class defines enough of the methods __lt__() , __le__() , __gt__() , and __ge__() (in general, __lt__() and __eq__() are sufficient, if you want the conventional meanings of the comparison operators).
sourceRefs.textQuote: Instances of a class cannot be ordered with respect to other instances of the same class, or other types of object, unless the class defines enough of the methods __lt__() , __le__() , __gt__() , and __ge__() (in general

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The behavior of the is and is not operators cannot be customized; also they can be applied to any two objects and never raise an exception.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The behavior of the is and is not operators cannot be customized; also they can be applied to any two objects and never raise an exception.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Two more operations with the same syntactic priority, in and not in , are supported by types that are iterable or implement the __contains__() method.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Two more operations with the same syntactic priority, in and not in , are supported by types that are iterable or implement the __contains__() method.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: There are three distinct numeric types: integers , floating-point numbers , and complex numbers . In addition, Booleans are a subtype of integers. Integers have unlimited precision. Floating-point numbers are usually implemented using double in C; information about the precision and internal representation of floating-point numbers for the machine on which y
sourceRefs.textQuote: There are three distinct numeric types: integers , floating-point numbers , and complex numbers . In addition, Booleans are a subtype of integers. Integers have unlimited precision. Floating-point numbers are usually imp

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Numbers are created by numeric literals or as the result of built-in functions and operators. Unadorned integer literals (including hex, octal and binary numbers) yield integers. Numeric literals containing a decimal point or an exponent sign yield floating-point numbers. Appending 'j' or 'J' to a numeric literal yields an imaginary number (a complex number
sourceRefs.textQuote: Numbers are created by numeric literals or as the result of built-in functions and operators. Unadorned integer literals (including hex, octal and binary numbers) yield integers. Numeric literals containing a decimal poi

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The constructors int() , float() , and complex() can be used to produce numbers of a specific type.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The constructors int() , float() , and complex() can be used to produce numbers of a specific type.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Python fully supports mixed arithmetic: when a binary arithmetic operator has operands of different built-in numeric types, the operand with the “narrower” type is widened to that of the other:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Python fully supports mixed arithmetic: when a binary arithmetic operator has operands of different built-in numeric types, the operand with the “narrower” type is widened to that of the other:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: If both arguments are complex numbers, no conversion is performed;
sourceRefs.textQuote: If both arguments are complex numbers, no conversion is performed;

sourceRefs.fallbackText: if either argument is a complex or a floating-point number, the other is converted to a floating-point number;
sourceRefs.textQuote: if either argument is a complex or a floating-point number, the other is converted to a floating-point number;

sourceRefs.fallbackText: otherwise, both must be integers and no conversion is necessary.
sourceRefs.textQuote: otherwise, both must be integers and no conversion is necessary.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Arithmetic with complex and real operands is defined by the usual mathematical formula, for example:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Arithmetic with complex and real operands is defined by the usual mathematical formula, for example:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: x + complex ( u , v ) = complex ( x + u , v ) x * complex ( u , v ) = complex ( x * u , x * v )
sourceRefs.textQuote: x + complex ( u , v ) = complex ( x + u , v ) x * complex ( u , v ) = complex ( x * u , x * v )

sourceRefs.fallbackText: A comparison between numbers of different types behaves as though the exact values of those numbers were being compared. [ 2 ]
sourceRefs.textQuote: A comparison between numbers of different types behaves as though the exact values of those numbers were being compared. [ 2 ]
